item.title: Imran Khan handed a prison sentence of 4 years and 3 months
item.description: Imran Khan handed a prison sentence of 4 years and 3 months

item.title: Minister for Finance Esrom Immanuel to appear on fijivillage Straight Talk at 7pm Thursday
item.description: Minister for Finance, Esrom Immanuel will appear on a special fijivillage Straight Talk With Vijay Narayan at 7pm this Thursday. The show will focus on the 2026/2027 National Budget.
item.content:encoded:
Minister for Finance, Esrom Immanuel will appear on a special fijivillage Straight Talk With Vijay Narayan at 7pm this Thursday.

The show will focus on the 2026/2027 National Budget.

You can watch the show on our facebook page, YouTube channel, TikTok and X account, fijivillage.

You can also catch the podcast, fijivillage Straight Talk With Vijay Narayan on Spotify and Apple Podcasts.

item.title: Father sentenced to 2 years and 8 months for son’s manslaughter, to serve 6 months imprisonment
item.description: 38-year-old Savenaca Damuni, the father of the late six-year-old boy, has been sentenced to 2 years and 8 months imprisonment, but

item.title: 5% tourism tax was introduced after consultation with Tourism Action Group - Goundar
item.description: Permanent Secretary for Finance, Shiri Goundar says the new 5 percent tourism services tax has been introduced following consultat

item.title: Cost of living and youth opportunities among key community concerns - Bryce Street resident
item.description: A community leader says the rising cost of living, the growth of informal settlements, support for farmers and opportunities for young people remain some of the biggest issues facing many Fijian families. Manasa Duvaga, who lives in the Bryce Street community in Raiwaqa and is originally from Dakuinuku Village in Tailevu, says many families continue to struggle with the rising cost of food, transp
item.content:encoded:
A community leader says the rising cost of living, the growth of informal settlements, support for farmers and opportunities for young people remain some of the biggest issues facing many Fijian families.

Manasa Duvaga, who lives in the Bryce Street community in Raiwaqa and is originally from Dakuinuku Village in Tailevu, says many families continue to struggle with the rising cost of food, transport and housing.

He says small and medium-sized businesses remain the backbone of Fiji's economy and should continue to receive support.

Duvaga also believes more attention needs to be given to informal settlements, saying many social issues, including drug-related problems, are affecting these communities.

He says transport subsidies and social welfare assistance continue to play an important role in helping families facing financial hardship.

Duvaga is also calling for greater support for agriculture, saying farmers and industries such as dairy need more assistance to improve production and create employment.

He says young people need more opportunities through sports, agriculture and small business programmes, to help keep them engaged and reduce the risk of becoming involved in drugs and crime.

Duvaga also raised concerns about the lack of village by-laws, that stronger community rules could help improve discipline and order.

He says many people continue to move from rural areas to towns and cities in search of work and education, but often end up living in informal settlements because affordable housing remains out of reach.

Duvaga is encouraging everyone to work together and continue praying for Fiji as the country moves forward.

item.title: Police deny FLP claim of special security at Minister Charan Jeath Singh's farm
item.description: Police have denied claims by the Fiji Labour Party that a police caravan has been set up outside the sugar cane farm of Minister for Public Enterprises Charan Jeath Singh in Waiqele, Labasa. This comes as FLP questioned whether Singh is being given special protection at taxpayers’ expense when there are over 3,000 cane farms in Labasa.
item.content:encoded:
Police have denied claims by the Fiji Labour Party that a police caravan has been set up outside the sugar cane farm of Minister for Public Enterprises Charan Jeath Singh in Waiqele, Labasa.

This comes as FLP questioned whether Singh is being given special protection at taxpayers’ expense when there are over 3,000 cane farms in Labasa.

They ask that if it is so, why is special privilege being granted to the Minister by the Fiji Police Force.

FLP says three police officers are reportedly on duty there 24/7 for a month now and asks how much is this costing the taxpayer.

They say Police Commissioner Rusiate Tudravu must tell the farmers of Labasa why this special protection is accorded to the Minister at taxpayers’ expense for a farm that is his personal property and not owned by the government.

The party also demands that Tudravu tell the nation why the police is not out in force keeping similar watch over the farms of other farmers in Labasa.

FLP adds that Charan Jeet Singh is a prosperous businessman and can afford to hire a private security firm to guard his farm if he is so worried.

While responding to questions by fijivillage News, police say the caravan is in the compound where a Police officer is renting.

They say police officers are patrolling the area due to increased number of cane burning.

Police add the caravan is a resting place for officers who are patrolling the sugar cane fields and it’s in a compound where they can access water and bathroom facility.

item.title: Fiji's maritime security boosted with new patrol boats from Japan
item.description: Fiji's maritime security will receive a significant boost with the addition of a new fleet of fast inshore patrol boats, enhancing the Fiji Navy's ability to protect the nation's maritime borders and respond to operational demands. The patrol boats were officially handed over by the Government of Japan during a ceremony attended by the Minister for Defence and Veterans Affairs , Pio Tikoduadua , a
item.content:encoded:
Fiji's maritime security will receive a significant boost with the addition of a new fleet of fast inshore patrol boats, enhancing the Fiji Navy's ability to protect the nation's maritime borders and respond to operational demands.

The patrol boats were officially handed over by the Government of Japan during a ceremony attended by the Minister for Defence and Veterans Affairs , Pio Tikoduadua , and Japan's Ambassador to Fiji, Hiroshi Tajima.

Tikoduadua says the new vessels will enhance the Fiji Navy's ability to conduct coastal surveillance, maritime patrols, search and rescue missions, and support whole-of-government operations across Fiji's extensive maritime domain.

Meanwhile, Fiji Navy expresses its sincere gratitude to the Government and people of Japan for their continued partnership and support through the Official Security Assistance framework.

The Fiji Navy says this valuable investment reinforces Fiji's ability to safeguard its sovereignty, strengthen maritime security, and ensure the safety of all who depend on the nation's waters.

item.title: 91-year-old fish seller says staying active is the secret to a long life
item.description: At 91 years old, Joji Rosinitoba Kean continues to sell fresh fish, a business he has been involved in for more than 20 years. He says staying active and maintaining a positive outlook on life have helped him remain strong.
item.content:encoded:
At 91 years old, Joji Rosinitoba Kean continues to sell fresh fish, a business he has been involved in for more than 20 years.

He says staying active and maintaining a positive outlook on life have helped him remain strong.

Kean, who is from Nakasaleka, Kadavu, says he is grateful to God for giving him the health and strength to continue working despite his age.

He says while he experiences body aches, his faith has carried him through the years.

The 91-year-old says he is also thankful for the support of his six children, who help him with his fish-selling business.

Kean says his focus is to enjoy life and remain happy, adding that no one knows when their time will come.

Reflecting on the past, he says Fiji has changed significantly over the years and believes people no longer value life the way they once did.

He says the different jobs he held when he was younger taught him the importance of hard work, time management, and appreciating life.

Kean worked for Reddy Construction in Samabula for 29 years before returning to Kadavu, where he started farming kava.

He also shared a message for young people, saying they are fortunate to have access to free education, but many do not take the opportunity seriously.

Kean says education is the key to success, while ignorance leads to failure.

item.title: Leung calls for no immunity for future coup perpetrators, backs secular state and electoral reforms
item.description: Former Attorney General Graham Leung has recommended that the Constitution should ensure future coup perpetrators are not granted
